stimuli.description: Red leftward arrow (stop signal overlaying the go_left arrow)
Red rightward arrow (stop signal overlaying the go_right arrow)
White leftward arrow with black border (go trial; correct response = ArrowLeft)
White rightward arrow with black border (go trial; correct response = ArrowRight)

phase_detection.complete: !!document.querySelector('#jspsych-download-as-text-link') || ((document.body.textContent || '').includes('Thank you for your participation') && (typeof jsPsych !== 'undefined') && jsPsych.progress().percent_complete >= 100)
phase_detection.practice: (typeof jsPsych !== 'undefined') && (() => { const v = jsPsych.data.get().filter({trial_type: 'custom-stop-signal-plugin'}).values(); return v.length > 0 && v[v.length - 1].block_i === 0; })()
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { const t = document.body.textContent || ''; return t.includes('GO TRIALS:') || t.includes('STOP-SIGNAL TRIALS:') || t.includes('correct response') || t.includes('incorrect response') || t.includes('correct stop') || t.includes('remember: try to stop') || t.includes('too slow') || t.includes('too fast') || t.includes('Press space to begin') || t.includes('Get ready') || t.includes('Thank you for your participation'); })()

timing.response_window_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('img#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!img) return false; const src = img.src || ''; return src.includes('go_') || src.includes('stop_'); })()
timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('img#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!img) return ''; const src = img.src || ''; const m = src.match(/(fix|go_left|go_right|stop_left|stop_right)/); return m ? m[1] : ''; })()

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.table_or_figure: Table 2 (go RT ex-Gaussian parameter estimates across datasets)
citations.quote: Posterior means for mu of the go-RT ex-Gaussian distribution ranged across participants and datasets between approximately 350 and 500 ms.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A.R., Band, G.P.H., Beste, C., Bissett, P.G., Brockett, A.T., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean correct go RT in healthy adult samples completing STOP-IT is in the 500-600 ms range, with substantial right-skew supporting ex-Gaussian decomposition.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.table_or_figure: Table 1; pp. 805-808 (mu reflects modal/leading edge of RT distribution)
citations.quote: The Gaussian component mu of the ex-Gaussian indexes the leading edge of the RT distribution and tracks non-decision and early decision processes.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian sigma estimates for go RTs typically fell between roughly 25 and 70 ms across the stop-signal datasets analyzed.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma captures Gaussian-distributed variability around the leading edge of the RT distribution.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian tau estimates for go RTs typically fell between roughly 70 and 180 ms across the stop-signal datasets analyzed.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S.J., & Mewhort, D.J.K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau captures the exponential tail of the distribution and is typically in the 70-200 ms range for choice RT.

go_left.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the standard family for stop-signal go RTs (Matzke & Wagenmakers, Heathcote). For healthy young adults in STOP-IT-style arrow tasks, mean go RT is ~530-550 ms; partitioning into mu~430, sigma~40, tau~110 yields a mean near 540 ms with right-skew consistent with published fits. Left/right go conditions are symmetric, so identical parameters.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Left and right go responses to arrow stimuli are not analyzed separately; ex-Gaussian parameters are reported pooled across response side.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Left and right go trials are balanced and treated as symmetric in the recommended choice stop-signal design.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for go RTs are reported pooled across response side.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Tau estimates for go RTs are reported pooled across response side; left/right symmetry is assumed.

go_right.rationale: Symmetric with go_left; no published evidence for left/right asymmetry in stop-signal arrow paradigms. Mean ~540 ms, sigma/tau as for go_left.

citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: The independent race model predicts that responses on signal-respond (failed-stop) trials are drawn from the leading edge of the go RT distribution and are therefore systematically faster than mean go RT.

citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: Empirical signal-respond RTs are reliably faster than mean go RTs, with typical differences of 30-80 ms in healthy adults.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean signal-respond RT should be less than mean go RT; violations indicate context-independence assumption is violated.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: The signal-respond distribution is a left-censored sub-distribution of the go RT distribution; Gaussian-component variability is compressed relative to unconditional go RTs.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Because signal-respond RTs are drawn from the fast tail of the go RT distribution, the exponential component tau is compressed relative to the unconditional go RT.

stop.rationale: Failed-stop (signal-respond) RTs are faster than matched go RTs by ~40-70 ms — a core race-model prediction (Logan & Cowan 1984; Verbruggen & Logan 2009). Shifting mu down ~60 ms and slightly compressing tau yields mean ~465 ms, consistent with reported signal-respond RT < mean go RT in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Short-range autoregressive coefficients estimated from choice-RT time series typically fall in the 0.05 to 0.25 range.

citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Trial-to-trial dependencies in RT include both short-range autoregressive structure (lag-1 correlations < 0.30) and long-range 1/f structure.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation is a general property of speeded-choice tasks (Wagenmakers, Farrell & Ratcliff 2004; Gilden 2001). Phi~0.15 reflects the short-range positive dependency typically observed in stop-signal go RTs; values >0.25 are rare.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean go RT across the four experimental blocks of STOP-IT is generally stable; systematic monotonic drift is not a prominent feature of the block-level data.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J.A., Cohen, J.R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R.A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Across stop-signal sessions of comparable length, block-wise mean go RT did not show a consistent linear drift across participants.

fatigue_drift.rationale: STOP-IT is brief (~280 trials, ~10 min) with frequent breaks between blocks. Verbruggen et al. (2019) report stable mean RT across the 4 experimental blocks; a monotonic per-trial drift is not the dominant temporal structure here. Practice (block-level) and post-event slowing capture the documented dynamics.

condition_repetition.rationale: Repetition priming for arrow direction in stop-signal is not consistently reported as a primary effect in this paradigm; SSD staircasing and stop-signal proportion are the dominant trial-level structure. Leaving disabled avoids introducing unwarranted modulation.

citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.table_or_figure: RT time-series spectra; estimated low-frequency power
citations.quote: The amplitude of the 1/f component in choice-RT series is small relative to total RT variance, typically contributing on the order of 5-20 ms of additional trial-by-trial structure.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: ARFIMA decompositions suggest a small but reliable 1/f component superimposed on AR(1) structure in choice RT.

citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Spectral slope alpha estimates in cognitive RT time series cluster near 1.0 (true 1/f), with empirical values typically between 0.7 and 1.2.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated long-memory exponents in RT series are consistent with alpha values near 1.0.

pink_noise.rationale: Long-range 1/f structure in RT exists (Gilden 2001) but overlaps substantially with the enabled autocorrelation mechanism at this trial count (~280). To avoid double-counting short-range temporal dependency, only the AR(1) mechanism is enabled.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G.D., Liefooghe, B., & Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Repetition priming or between-trial control adjustments?
citations.quote: Aftereffects of stop signals in the stop-signal paradigm are well-captured by post-event (post-stop) slowing rather than by a condition-by-condition transition table, and no congruency-sequence-style interaction is documented.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Sequential trial-type effects in the stop-signal task are dominated by the post-stop slowing of go RTs, not by interactions of consecutive go-trial sub-conditions.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Stop-signal does not have a documented condition-sequence interaction analogous to congruency-sequence in conflict tasks. The well-documented sequence effects (post-error slowing, post-stop slowing) are event-triggered and handled by post_event_slowing, not by transition-table modulation.

citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Reaction times on trials immediately following errors were systematically longer than RTs on trials following correct responses, demonstrating a robust post-error slowing effect.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H.L.J., Forstmann, B.U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes in speeded choice tasks typically range from approximately 20 to 80 ms.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.table_or_figure: Experiments 1-3 (RT after stop vs. no-stop trials)
citations.quote: Go RTs on trials following any stop-signal trial are reliably slowed by roughly 20-90 ms relative to go RTs following another go trial, regardless of whether inhibition succeeded.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G.D., Liefooghe, B., & Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Repetition priming or between-trial control adjustments?
citations.quote: Post-stop slowing is observed after both successful and failed stop trials and reflects a between-trial control adjustment.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Two distinct event-triggered slowing effects are documented for stop-signal: (a) classical post-error slowing on the trial after a go-trial error (~30-60 ms; Rabbitt 1966; Dutilh et al. 2012), and (b) post-stop-signal slowing on the trial after any stop trial, regardless of stop outcome (~40-90 ms; Bissett & Logan 2011; Verbruggen, Logan, Liefooghe & Vandierendonck 2008). The interrupt trigger is marked exclusive_with_prior_triggers so a stop trial that the bot failed to inhibit does not double-add the error and interrupt slowing.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Participants typically speed up by roughly 20-60 ms between the practice/first experimental block and the asymptotic later blocks of STOP-IT.

citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Mean go RT in the practice block of stop-signal tasks is approximately 30-50 ms longer than mean go RT in the experimental blocks.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Block-level mean go RT typically plateaus by the second or third experimental block of STOP-IT.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: The recommended STOP-IT design uses 4 experimental blocks of 64 trials each.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Most of the early-session RT reduction in STOP-IT occurs within the first experimental block, consistent with a rapid exponential decay of the practice offset.

practice_effect.rationale: STOP-IT participants typically speed up ~30-60 ms from the early practice/first-experimental block to asymptote, plateauing by the 2nd-3rd experimental block (Verbruggen et al. 2019; general stop-signal practice literature). Initial offset of +45 ms decaying to zero by block 2 reflects this without overstating; decay_rate 0.55 yields most of the speedup in the first block.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: The brief, block-structured STOP-IT session (~10 min, 4 blocks separated by breaks) does not produce the kinds of vigilance decrements documented in extended continuous tasks such as the PVT.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: STOP-IT is short and block-structured with explicit breaks; vigilance decrement (increasing variability over sustained uninterrupted attention) is documented for longer continuous tasks (e.g., PVT, SART), not for stop-signal of this length. Leaving disabled.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean go RT in healthy young adults on stop-signal is ~60-80 ms (Verbruggen et al. 2019 normative data; Congdon et al. 2012). Condition-level SD (~25 ms) captures the spread in the signal-respond minus go RT contrast across subjects. sigma_tau multiplicative jitter of ±15% reflects published variability in ex-Gaussian fit parameters across individuals. Go-trial accuracy is consistently >95% in healthy adults so accuracy_sd 0.03 with floor at 0.80 is conservative; omission rate is near-zero so SD 0.01 with cap at 0.05 is appropriate.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J.A., Cohen, J.R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R.A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Between-subject standard deviation of mean go RT in healthy young adult samples on stop-signal tasks is approximately 60-90 ms.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Inter-individual variability in mean go RT in STOP-IT samples is on the order of 60-80 ms SD.

citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.table_or_figure: Subject-level descriptives for signal-respond minus go RT
citations.quote: Inter-individual SD of the signal-respond minus go RT contrast is roughly 20-35 ms in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., Wiecki, T.V., Brown, S.D., Logan, G.D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Individual-level sigma and tau estimates vary by roughly ±10-20% around the group-level estimate across healthy adult participants.

citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial choice accuracy in healthy adult samples is consistently above 95%, with between-subject SD on the order of 2-4 percentage points.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Participants are typically excluded if go-trial choice accuracy falls below approximately 80%.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-trial omission (missed responses within the response window) is typically below 5% in healthy adults; subjects exceeding ~10% are commonly excluded.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: A go-trial choice-accuracy floor near 80% is the conventional inclusion threshold; the ceiling is 100%.

citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Participants with go-trial accuracy below approximately 80% were excluded from analysis.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Recommended exclusion threshold for go-trial omissions is approximately 10%; omission rates in retained subjects are typically below 5%.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'STOP-IT (jsPsych Stop Signal Task)' with 4 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 3 conditions; enabled 3 temporal effects.
Produced 46 citations across 27 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 33 parameters; 6 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed after 1 refinement(s): 22 trials matched, conditions ['go_left', 'go_right', 'stop'] all observed. See pilot_refinement_*.diff for changes the bot made.
